screens.body: Every technique in this course traces back to a small set of primary sources, and reading them in the right order matters. Start with **Gardner, Napolitano & Paura's "Cyclostationarity: Half a Century of Research" (2006, Signal Processing)** — a survey paper that orients you across the whole field before you commit to anything denser. From there, **William A. Gardner's "Statistical Spectral Analysis: A Nonprobabilistic Theory" (1987)** is the foundational text this course's core-theory module is built from — dense, but the source everything else cites. **Antonio Napolitano's "Cyclostationarity: New Trends and Applications" (2020)** is the modern follow-up, filling gaps Gardner's older book leaves. For the practical estimation algorithms (FAM/SSCA), **Roberts, Brown & Loomis, "Computationally Efficient Algorithms for Cyclic Spectral Analysis" (1991, IEEE SP Magazine)** is the reference. And ongoing: **Chad Spooner's CSP blog** is a genuinely useful bridge between textbook derivations and applied, worked examples — a good habit to build once the textbook material has landed.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: In your own words: why read the Gardner/Napolitano/Paura survey paper before attempting Gardner's 1987 book?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A strong answer notes that the survey paper is written to orient a reader across the whole field's history and structure at a much gentler pace, before diving into a dense foundational text — reading it first means Gardner's book is filling in depth on a map you already roughly have, rather than being your first exposure to the field's shape at all.

screens.front: Which paper is the reference for the FAM and SSCA cyclic-spectrum-estimation algorithms?
screens.back: Roberts, Brown & Loomis, "Computationally Efficient Algorithms for Cyclic Spectral Analysis" (1991, IEEE Signal Processing Magazine).
